other_observers: Kevin Groeneweg for sure. Aaron Mitchell and Curt VanBoening were also present but I don't recall if they saw this bird well. Kevin and I discussed the field marks as we observed the bird.; I think that Kevin and I saw it at the same time.
specific_location: approximately 1/3 of a mile east Two Buttes dam
description: This bird was with a group of other migrant passerines including Yellow Warblers and a Cassin's Vireo. Kevin Groeneweg and I observed the bird a couple of times for about 10 seconds each in bright sunlight with a favorable light angle. The bird was obviously of the Solitary Vireo complex, with prominent white "spectacles" and two distinct wingbars. When we first viewed it from the front, the clearly delineated edges of the white throat had us hoping for a Plumbeous, which is the rarest of this group in Kansas and always much hoped for there. However, as it turned to the side we were able to see that the blue-gray head plumage was cleanly separated from a bright green back. The green tertials and primaries were edged with yellow-gold, quite apparent given the good light conditions. Also the wingbars were yellow instead of white. The flanks were broadly smudged with bright yellow. So it was apparent to us that this was a typical Blue-headed such as we are quite familiar with in east-central Kansas. The presence of a Cassin's Vireo in the same tree offered a good comparison of these two birds.Behaviors: The vireo was part of a mixed-species flock of warblers and vireos that was moving through the middle canopy of the riparian corridor below Two Buttes dam. It was fairly active during the brief presence of this small flock of birds. We were unable to relocate either vireo when a couple of other members of our party caught up with us.Call: silentPlumage: adult
